items.rationale: Building Jolt as the fastest, simplest open-source zkVM to scale blockchains and get more computation onchain; his single most sustained technical obsession.

citations.quote: Today we're sharing our biggest open source release yet: Jolt Alpha, our zkVM built new from the ground up.
If you're very curious, you should join us in improving Jolt! It will always be free and open source

items.rationale: Sees private, decentralized ID (World ID) as essential infrastructure for sybil resistance, bot defense, and giving real humans priority as AI floods the internet.

citations.quote: How bad does this need to get before all networks need to integrate a private proof of personhood system like @worldnetwork?
using the privacy-preserving World ID (available only to humans) to give individuals priority access where needed.

items.rationale: Network tokens distribute ownership of decentralized networks and can capture protocol revenue; he calls them 'the king' and core to the industry's maturation.

citations.quote: Network tokens (the king) vs. company-backed tokens (not so much).
Network tokens like ETH, SOL, and UNI can capture value from their underlying networks through protocol fees.

items.rationale: Long-standing crypto goal whose pieces are 'finally coming together'; sees increasingly obvious product-market fit and expects more experimentation.

citations.quote: Payments has been one of the goals of crypto from the beginning. The pieces are finally coming together.
With increasingly obvious product-market fit for stablecoins, I expect more experiments here

items.rationale: Views games as a historical source of software innovation; cites Proof of Play topping throughput charts and active EVE Frontier collaboration.

citations.quote: Narrative violation: #1 chain measured by Mgas/s (the correct measure for throughput) is @ProofOfPlay — by a long shot.
it's been great collaborating with him on EVE Frontier!

items.rationale: Believes an internet of mostly generated content requires cryptographic provenance, attestation, and trust graphs — solvable only with blockchains.
items.age_note: Stated most intensely 2023-2024; not restated verbatim recently, but the adjacent identity/personhood thesis persists into Feb 2025.

citations.quote: Every robust method to prove the origin or history of content involves cryptography and blockchains.
The 'mystery meat' scenario seems unavoidable

items.rationale: Pushing Helios to give Ethereum and L2s real in-browser light-client verification by default; wants RPC providers to serve proofs.

citations.quote: You can run a Helios light client in the browser to verify Ethereum L1 + OP Mainnet + Base. It works right now.

items.rationale: Argues autonomous AI agents can't have bank accounts but can hold crypto wallets and pay each other and for their own compute.

citations.quote: AI agents can't have bank accounts, but they can have crypto wallets.
agents being able to pay each other, and even for their own compute ... It will definitely happen.

items.rationale: Excited about a protocol to measure impact, attribute credit and route payment to open-source authors — completing 'Git's evolution.'

citations.quote: Merit Systems was founded to design this: a protocol for measuring impact, attributing credit, and routing payment to the authors creating value

items.rationale: Champions symbolic/invariant testing; highlights formally verifying Ethereum's Pectra contracts and Uniswap v4 components with little code.

citations.quote: We formally verified Ethereum's new Pectra system contracts — using Halmos — with <1000 lines of code.

items.rationale: Argues closed-source limits innovation and 'safety' secrecy causes no identifiable harm; praises open uncensored models and DeepSeek-R1's visible reasoning.

citations.quote: arguments that GPT-4 and even GPT-3 were unsafe to open source aren't believable.
absolutely zero harm comes from seeing DeepSeek-R1's reasoning.

items.rationale: Wants Ethereum to raise blob targets and smooth pricing; founders pick infra on expected capacity and Ethereum 'cannot afford to slow down.'

citations.quote: Ethereum should raise the blob target to 4 and nudge the calldata cost ... Ethereum cannot afford to slow down.

items.rationale: Believes the casino sets crypto back — deterring great founders, arming politicized regulators, and offering nothing technically interesting.
items.age_note: Condemned most forcefully April 2024; by 2025 he channels the concern into rigorous token taxonomy rather than repeated denunciation, but the underlying stance persists.

citations.quote: we shouldn't pretend that the casino doesn't set us back. Deterring great founders and giving ammo to politicized regulators has a profound effect.
memecoins aren't very technically interesting.

items.rationale: Warns that tokens controlled by a single company with no path to decentralization are not network tokens — likely securities posing holder risk.

citations.quote: If there is only one company with actual control of the system ... this is not a (decentralized) network token. It is something else
'Utility tokens' aren't really a category. 'Adding utility' doesn't change a token's classification

items.rationale: Expects most content to become assumed-generated ('mystery meat') without cryptographic provenance, eroding trust online.
items.age_note: Heavily argued 2023-early 2024; framed as the problem his identity/provenance excitement addresses.

citations.quote: Eventually all content is assumed generated unless accompanied by the requisite provenance/signatures/attestations/proofs.

items.rationale: Considers CAPTCHA defeated and LLMs able to fabricate realistic histories, making sybil resistance and proof-of-personhood urgent.

citations.quote: CAPTCHA is done and LLMs today are already sophisticated enough to produce long and realistic looking 'histories'

items.rationale: Skeptical of secrecy-as-safety; sees closed models as drastically limiting startup, academic and independent innovation.

citations.quote: Closed source drastically limits innovation from startups, universities, independent researchers, etc. And it makes it more difficult to keep software safe.

items.rationale: Flagged systematic debanking of crypto startups in late 2024 — but by Jan 2025 he treats the regulatory environment as transformed for the better.
items.age_note: Largely reversed: late-2024 debanking concern gave way to Jan 2025 optimism ('the regulatory environment has changed completely').

citations.quote: Why are your priors telling you this did not happen? Respectfully, they may need adjusting
the regulatory environment has changed completely. This is it.

items.rationale: Archetypal network token that can capture protocol fees as revenue; he also pushes its scaling roadmap (blobs).

citations.quote: Network tokens like ETH, SOL, and UNI can capture value from their underlying networks through protocol fees.

items.rationale: Cited as a genuine network token able to capture fees via mechanisms like UniStaker once the fee switch turns on.

citations.quote: UNI can capture fees from the network (the switch is off right now) via a mechanism like UniStaker.

items.rationale: Grouped with ETH and UNI as a legitimate network token whose value derives from a decentralized network, not a single company.

citations.quote: Many companies build on and use Ethereum, Solana, and Uniswap — but they're using the network, and none of them control it.

items.rationale: Rejects the 'bitcoin is a memecoin' framing — its position as native asset of the most secure decentralized network gives it unique economic characteristics.

citations.quote: being the native asset of ... the most secure and decentralized network gives it economic characteristics ... unlike any memecoin. So I don't think it's a memecoin.

items.rationale: Referenced approvingly as a stablecoin whose system is the smart contracts governing issuance/redemption; rides his broader stablecoin PMF thesis.

citations.quote: In the case of stablecoins like USDC, the system is the smart contracts that control the issuance/redemption of the token

items.rationale: Calls out RAI as a high-potential, under-appreciated non-pegged algorithmic stablecoin model and expects more experiments in the space.

citations.quote: Is RAI still the only non-fiat (including price peg) algorithmic stablecoin? ... I expect more experiments here (no LUNA please).

items.rationale: Pure-meme tokens with no enterprise value or protocol revenue; he treats them as net-negative for the industry when that's 'all there is.'

citations.quote: memecoins/store of value tokens like Dogecoin have no enterprise value (like gold).
It's a memecoin when that's all there is.

items.rationale: Invoked as the cautionary failure mode for new algorithmic-stablecoin experiments — explicitly 'no LUNA please.'

citations.quote: I expect more experiments here (no LUNA please).

risk_regime.rationale: As an a16z crypto builder/CTO he is structurally bullish on building — 'never been a better time to start a project in crypto,' citing better tech, clearer need, and a transformed regulatory environment. His risk-on posture is toward fundamental network-building, explicitly NOT toward speculative casino/memecoin trading, which he condemns.

notes: Eddy's worldview is technical and structural, not tactical/price-driven — he makes essentially no market-timing calls. Recency weighting matters most in two areas: (1) the 2024 memecoin condemnation evolved by 2025 into rigorous network-vs-company-backed token taxonomy (same underlying concern, more analytical framing); (2) the late-2024 crypto-debanking concern reversed into Jan 2025 regulatory optimism. His most durable, highest-conviction throughlines are ZK/zkVMs (Jolt), decentralized private identity/proof-of-personhood, sound network-token design, and the cryptographic-provenance answer to AI-generated content. Token-level views are framed as classification (what makes a legitimate network token) rather than buy/sell conviction; conviction scores reflect his enthusiasm for the model, not a price target.
